1.exposition: The preparation is over; now the wrath is poured. A great voice from the temple — from God himself, since no one could enter while the sanctuary was filled with smoke — commands the seven angels, Go and pour out the bowls of the wrath of God upon the earth. The first four fall in swift succession on the four spheres of creation: land, sea, fresh waters, and sun. Painful sores break out on the beast's worshippers (v.2); sea and rivers turn to blood (vv.3–4); the sun is given power to scorch with fire (v.8). The judgment is neither random nor cruel: it strikes precisely those who bore the mark, and the angel of the waters and the altar break in with a doxology owning God 'just' (vv.5–7). Yet the section closes on the terrible refrain of the impenitent: they blasphemed and did not repent.
1.original_language: The bowls are steeped in the plagues of Egypt, but the limits are gone. Where the trumpets struck 'a third' (Revelation 8:7), the bowls (phialai, the shallow libation-saucers of the temple) are emptied whole. The first sore is an helkos kakon kai ponēron — 'a bad and malignant ulcer,' the same word the Septuagint uses for the boils of Exodus 9:10. The sea becomes 'blood as of a dead man' (haima hōs nekrou) — not fresh blood but the dark, clotted gore of a corpse, echoing the Nile of Exodus 7:20. And of the persecutors it is said, with a beautiful abruptness, axioi eisin — 'they are worthy,' the exact-measure verdict of divine justice.
1.historical_context: John's persecuted readers would recognize the pattern at once: the God who broke Pharaoh to free his people is breaking the beast's empire by the same signs — boils (Exodus 9:10), water turned to blood (Exodus 7:20), scorching and darkness. But where the Exodus plagues were meant to bend Pharaoh toward release, the bowls fall after the door of warning has shut. A Roman world well acquainted with plague and with blood spilled in the arena would feel the force of sores on the mark-bearers and seas fouled with gore. The older expositors preserved here — Barnes, Gill, Clarke — divide over whether the strokes are literal or figurative, several reading them as the successive blows by which a corrupt Christendom would fall; but all agree the unrepentant blasphemy is the settled character of those who have sworn allegiance to the beast.
1.christ: The Lamb who alone was found worthy to open the scroll (Revelation 5:9) is the one whose sealed wrath is now unstopped. The justice the altar celebrates — blood for those who shed blood — is the very justice that fell at the cross, where Christ drank the cup of wrath in his people's place (Matthew 26:39). So the motif of blood runs two ways: the blood of the Lamb that washes robes white (Revelation 7:14), and the blood given to drink to those who spilled the saints'. The same hand offers the cup of salvation and pours the cup of judgment; faith in him is the whole difference between the two.

verses.note: The wrath begins not in chaos but at command: a great voice orders the angels to pour out the bowls of the wrath of God upon the earth. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes note the voice can only be God's, since none could enter the smoke-filled temple; heaven answers the martyrs' long 'how long?' puritan Matthew Henry marks the obedience — where Moses and Jeremiah drew back from their errands, the angels move at once, the will of God done on earth as in heaven. latin-fathers Bede, reading allegorically, hears in the outpoured bowls the church's Spirit-given word that both warns and condemns the ungodly.

The first bowl falls upon the earth, and a foul, painful sore breaks out — but only on those who bore the mark of the beast and worshipped his image. grammatical-historical Clarke hears a direct echo of the sixth Egyptian plague, the boils of Exodus 9:10; Ellicott adds that Egypt is the type of the world-power, so the stroke need not be literal so much as a throbbing, hateful affliction of those who serve the oppressor. The mark they chose becomes the mark God's judgment finds.

The second bowl turns the sea to blood as of a dead man, and every living soul in it died. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the vivid horror — not fresh blood but coagulated corpse-gore — and that where the trumpet destroyed only a third (Revelation 8:9), here the death is total; the first Egyptian plague (Exodus 7:20) is behind it. wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads it plainly: the sea, congealed and putrid, kills every creature in it.

The third bowl extends the bloody judgment from sea to the rivers and fountains of waters, and they became blood — the very springs of drink. grammatical-historical Clarke again traces the first Egyptian plague (Exodus 7:20), citing the rabbinic maxim that the plagues God laid on Egypt he lays on the enemies of his people in every age. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the streams feed the sea: when the great deep is corrupt, the fresh springs that flow into it are corrupted too. The retribution is not arbitrary but declared in the verse that follows.

Heaven interprets the plague before earth can misjudge it. grammatical-historical Barnes explains 'the angel of the waters' by the old belief that the elements are set under angelic charge (cf. Revelation 7:1); this one owns God righteous, who art and wast and shalt be, because thou hast judged thus. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the eternity-title of Revelation 1:4 and the added 'thou Holy One' (hosios): amid revolutions that turn rivers to blood, the mind turns to the unchanging God whose throne endures.

The ground of the sentence: they shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott hear the martyrs' cry of Revelation 6:10 answered at last — the exact lex talionis of God, blood for blood. grammatical-historical Clarke sharpens it with a grim classical figure: as Tomyris plunged the head of Cyrus into a skin of blood, saying 'Sate thyself with the blood thou didst thirst for,' so the blood-thirsty are made to drink their fill.

A second voice confirms the first: Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments. grammatical-historical Barnes hears it come 'out of the altar' — perhaps the very altar beneath which the martyred souls had cried (Revelation 6:9), now testifying that their blood is avenged. It is the same doxology sung before the bowls began (Revelation 15:3): heaven twice over vindicates the justice of God, so that no reader may mistake severity for injustice.

The fourth bowl is poured on the sun, and power was given it to scorch men with fire. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes note the passive 'was given' — even this fierce heat is by divine permission, not accident. puritan Matthew Henry marks the irony of it: the sun, the great giver of light and life, is turned into an instrument of torment against those who love darkness. What should have blessed them now burns.

And here the plagues meet the wall of a hardened heart: men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God, and repented not to give him glory. grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill note the strange logic — even blaspheming, they confess that God 'has power over these plagues,' yet hate rather than bow. It is the same verdict pronounced after the trumpets (Revelation 9:20): suffering alone does not soften; only grace does. Judgment poured on a rebel heart draws out curses, not tears.

witnesses.point: The great voice can only be God's, since none could enter the smoke-filled temple; the plagues echo Egypt, and each falls on the followers of the beast.
The sea 'as the blood of a dead man' is coagulated corpse-gore, and the destruction is total where the trumpet's was only a third.
The bowls allude throughout to the Egyptian plagues — boils and blood — on the rabbinic principle that God repeats Egypt's plagues on his people's enemies in every age.
The angels obey God's command instantly, unlike Moses or Jeremiah; the sun that should bless is turned to torment against those who worship the beast.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: Reads the bowls against the Exodus plagues as figurative depictions of God's justice on the oppressor rather than a literal catalogue of catastrophes; the imagery undoes creation's blessings for those who have given themselves to the beast.

1.application: Two responses stand side by side in this passage: heaven, which answers judgment with worship, and the impenitent, who answer it with cursing. The difference is not the severity of the trouble but the state of the heart that meets it. Ask which voice your own suffering has taught you to speak.

10.exposition: Now the wrath strikes at the seat of power itself. The fifth bowl is poured on the throne of the beast, and his kingdom was plunged into darkness; men gnaw their tongues in anguish yet still curse God and will not repent. The sixth dries up the great Euphrates to prepare the way of the kings from the east, and three unclean spirits like frogs issue from the mouths of the dragon, the beast, and the false prophet, working signs to gather the kings of the whole world to the battle of the great day of God Almighty — to a place called Armageddon. Judgment now reaches the center of the antichristian order, and the powers of evil are drawn, as by a hook, toward their own undoing. Into the middle of that mustering breaks a single, startling voice — Christ's own — with a beatitude for the watchful (v.15).
10.original_language: The name in v.16 is the interpreter's great crux. Greek Harmagedōn renders a Hebrew phrase, most naturally Har-Megiddo, 'the mountain (or hill) of Megiddo.' The difficulty is that Megiddo is a mound on a plain, not a mountain — which drives some to hear instead a symbolic coinage, perhaps har mo'ed, 'the mount of assembly,' or an allusion to the mountains of Israel where the nations are destroyed. The 'frogs' (batrachoi) are Levitically unclean and recall the second Egyptian plague (Exodus 8:6); the 'kings from the rising of the sun' (apo anatolēs hēliou) evoke the dreaded hordes beyond the eastern frontier.
10.historical_context: Every image here is loaded with memory. The darkness on the beast's throne recalls the ninth plague, when even Pharaoh's court went black (Exodus 10:21). The drying of the Euphrates evokes Cyrus, who diverted the river to take historical Babylon (Isaiah 44:27; Jeremiah 50:38), while 'the kings from the east' would summon the first-century dread of a Parthian invasion across that very border. And Megiddo was Israel's great killing-field — where Deborah's God swept away Sisera (Judges 5:19) and where good King Josiah fell (2 Kings 23:29; 2 Chronicles 35:22), so that its plain became a byword for national mourning (Zechariah 12:11). On the reference the witnesses divide honestly: some read a literal locality where the last battle is joined, while the Reformed and grammatical expositors (Henry, Barnes) more often take it as a symbol drawn from that historic battlefield — the name, not the map, doing the work — for the decisive clash in which the gathered powers are overthrown.
10.christ: The most arresting thing in the chapter is that, amid the war-drums, Christ himself speaks: Behold, I am coming like a thief; blessed is he who watches and keeps his garments. It is the very warning he gave the churches at Revelation 3:3 and on the Mount of Olives (Matthew 24:43; 1 Thessalonians 5:2). The kings gather against him, but 'the great day of God the Almighty' is the day of the Lamb's own appearing, when the Rider on the white horse strikes the nations (Revelation 19:11). The outcome is never in doubt; the saints' part is not to calculate the hour but to stay awake and keep on the robe of righteousness he supplies.

verses.note: The fifth bowl is aimed with precision at the throne of the beast, and his kingdom was full of darkness. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes note that the stroke falls not on the subjects only but on the very seat of power, echoing the ninth Egyptian plague (Exodus 10:21). grammatical-historical Ellicott hears in the darkness both literal calamity and the eclipse of the beast's authority — confusion so deep that men 'gnaw their tongues for pain,' torment and bewilderment together at the heart of the empire.

The response is the old refrain, now deepening: they blasphemed the God of heaven for their pains and their sores, and repented not of their deeds. grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill note that 'their sores' shows the first plague (v.2) still festering as the fifth falls — the judgments accumulate, and with them the hardness. They curse God for the very wounds their own sin has earned. The second sounding of 'repented not' (with v.9) binds the bowls into one verdict on the impenitent heart.

The sixth bowl dries the great river Euphrates, that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the drying as a barrier removed, as when the Red Sea and Jordan opened — but here the interpretations part honestly. The older historicists read the withering of the Ottoman power that long barred the East; the futurist expects literal kings marching to the final battle (v.14); and puritan Matthew Henry reads simply a providential clearing of the way for God's appointed purpose, whether the ingathering of the nations or the overthrow of Babylon. All agree God removes the obstacles to his own ends.

John sees three unclean spirits like frogs come out of the mouth of the dragon, and of the beast, and of the false prophet. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes note the unholy trinity that parodies Father, Son, and Spirit, and that the spirits issue from the 'mouth' — the lying speech and propaganda by which the three deceive. The frogs, loathsome and Levitically unclean, recall the second Egyptian plague (Exodus 8:6); as a lying spirit once lured Ahab to his fall (1 Kings 22:22), so these lure the world to Armageddon.

Their nature and errand are named: they are spirits of demons, working signs, that go to the kings of the whole world to gather them to the battle of the great day of God Almighty. grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott note the lying wonders (as Revelation 13:13; 2 Thessalonians 2:9) by which the nations are mustered. eastern Cyril of Alexandria marks the irony of the phrase: the powers gather for battle, but it is 'the great day of God Almighty' — the day of his triumph, not theirs. Evil assembles only to be crushed.

Suddenly, across the din of mustering armies, another voice: Behold, I come as a thief. Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments, lest he walk naked, and they see his shame. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson identify the speaker as Christ himself, breaking into the vision with the warning of Matthew 24:43 and 1 Thessalonians 5:2. Ellicott draws out the beatitude: blessed is the one who stays awake and keeps his garments — clothed in readiness and righteousness — lest the sudden Lord find him stripped and shamed. The pastoral heart of the chapter beats here.

The mustering reaches its place: he gathered them into a place called in the Hebrew tongue Armageddon. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson give the name as Har-Megiddo, the mountain of Megiddo, the historic battlefield of Deborah and of Josiah (Judges 5:19; 2 Kings 23:29; Zechariah 12:11). Here the readings divide plainly and should be held honestly: some take a literal locality where the last war is joined; more commonly the expositors read it as a symbol, the name of Israel's great slaughter-field standing for the decisive final conflict in which the gathered antichristian powers are overthrown.

witnesses.point: The frog-spirits issue from the mouths of the unholy trinity — dragon, beast, false prophet — as lying propaganda that gathers the kings; 'Armageddon' is Har-Megiddo, the historic battlefield.
The darkness on the beast's throne is both calamity and the eclipse of his power; the beatitude blesses the one who keeps his garments of readiness against the sudden coming.
The drying of the Euphrates is God's providential clearing of the way for his own purpose — whether the ingathering of the nations or the fall of Babylon.
The kings are gathered to 'the great day of God Almighty' — a day of God's triumph, not the beast's; evil assembles only for its own overthrow.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: Noting that Megiddo is a plain with no literal mountain, reads Har-Magedon as a symbol rather than a map coordinate — the eschatological gathering of the nations against God and their defeat, drawing on the prophetic pattern of the enemy massed against the mountains of Israel.

10.application: The world's powers converge for a war they are sure they will win, and in the same breath Christ turns to his church and says only: stay awake, keep your clothes on. When headlines read like the gathering at Armageddon, the believer's calling is not to decode the date but to be found watching, unashamed, clothed in the righteousness he gives.

17.exposition: The last angel pours his bowl into the air, and a great voice came out of the temple, from the throne, saying, It is done. The wrath is complete. Lightnings, voices, thunders, and the greatest earthquake in human history follow; the great city is split into three parts, the cities of the nations fall, and great Babylon is remembered before God to be given the cup of the wine of the fury of his wrath. Islands flee, mountains vanish, and hundred-pound hailstones fall — yet even now, under the last plague, men blaspheme God. The single word from the throne, 'It is done,' announces that God's appointed judgment has reached its end; and the naming of Babylon at the very close is a hinge, opening onto the two chapters that will unfold her fall in full (Revelation 17–18).
17.original_language: The voice from the throne says a single Greek word: gegonen — a perfect tense, 'it has come to pass,' 'it is done and stands done.' It is the wrath-side twin of the gegonan that will sound over the new creation, It is done — the same divine completion pronounced first of judgment, then of grace. And behind both lies the cry from the cross, tetelestai, 'It is finished' (John 19:30). Babylon is made to drain 'the cup of the wine of the fury of his wrath' (tou thymou tēs orgēs), doubling the words for anger to press the sheer weight of it — the settled wrath and the blazing passion of God against entrenched evil.
17.historical_context: 'Babylon the great' is Revelation's cipher for Rome — the city on seven hills that the next chapter will name outright (Revelation 17:9) — and beyond Rome, every self-worshipping world-order. The cup she must drink fulfills the threat already sounded in Revelation 14:10, and it is the same cup she forced on the nations (Revelation 14:8; Jeremiah 25:15). The talent-weight hail — stones of fifty to a hundred pounds — recalls the seventh Egyptian plague (Exodus 9:24), and the world-shaking earthquake echoes the prophets' Day of the LORD (Haggai 2:6; Zechariah 14:4). The city split 'into three parts' signals not damage but total collapse. To hearers who knew Rome's seemingly eternal power, the message is plain: the empire that makes itself God will be remembered by God — for judgment, not forever.
17.christ: 'It is done,' spoken from the throne, cannot be heard by a Christian without the echo of Golgotha, where Jesus cried 'It is finished' (John 19:30). The wrath consummated in the bowls is the very wrath the Son exhausted at the cross for all who trust him — he prayed that the cup might pass, then drained it to the dregs (Mark 14:36). The two utterances are the two faces of one finished work: redemption accomplished, judgment fulfilled. And the Christ who drank the cup is the one who now hands it to Babylon. His 'It is done' of wrath is the threshold of the greater 'It is done' of Revelation 21:6, when he wipes away every tear — for judgment is never God's last word to his people, only the doorway to the new creation.

verses.note: The seventh bowl is poured into the air, and a great voice from the throne declared, It is done. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the air as the all-encompassing element, perhaps the realm of the 'prince of the power of the air' (Ephesians 2:2), and hear in the announcement the completion of the wrath begun in Revelation 15:1. puritan Matthew Henry weighs the finality of the word: what God appointed against the antichristian world is now, at a stroke, accomplished.

The word is sealed with convulsion: voices, and thunders, and lightnings, and a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the storm-tokens of God's awful presence in judgment, the same that marked the throne and the opened temple (Revelation 4:5; Revelation 11:19). eastern Cyril of Alexandria marks the superlative — an upheaval beyond any before it, shaking the whole order of the antichristian world to its foundation.

The quake does its work: the great city was divided into three parts, the cities of the nations fell, and great Babylon came in remembrance before God, to give her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note that God 'remembers' Babylon's sins (Revelation 18:5) — covenant language for the arrival of her long-deferred reckoning. She who made the nations drink her wine of fornication (Revelation 14:8) now drinks the cup of God's fury (Jeremiah 25:15). The apparent delay was never amnesia.

Every island fled away, and the mountains were not found. grammatical-historical Robertson and Barnes read the sweeping figure, drawn from the sixth seal (Revelation 6:14): the most stable and permanent features of the earth — islands and mountains, the very emblems of what cannot be moved — are swept away. Nothing of the old order is left standing; the consummating judgment levels all, whether one reads a literal dissolution or the overthrow of the kingdoms it symbolizes.

The vials close as they ran: a great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent, fell upon men; and men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the crushing stones — fifty to a hundred pounds each — recalling the seventh Egyptian plague (Exodus 9:24) and the seventh trumpet (Revelation 11:19). And the final word on the human heart apart from grace is the refrain one last time (vv.9, 11): crushed to the very end, they blaspheme still.

witnesses.point: The seventh bowl into the air completes the wrath of 15:1; God 'remembers' Babylon's sins, bringing her to the long-deferred cup of his fury.
The thunders, lightnings, and earthquake are the tokens of God's presence in judgment; the city split in three signals total collapse, and the hail recalls Egypt's plague.
The earthquake is a convulsion beyond any since men were on the earth — the whole antichristian order shaken to its foundation.
'It is done' is the word of finality: what God appointed against the antichristian world is now, at a stroke, accomplished.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: Hears in 'It is done' (gegonen) the announcement that God's wrath is complete, deliberately paired with the 'It is done' of new creation at 21:6 — the same divine completion pronounced first of judgment and then of salvation, so that the end of the bowls opens directly toward the renewal of all things.

17.application: 'It is done' is a fearful word here and a healing word two cries earlier, from a cross. The same completion that will one day shut Babylon's account was already spoken over your sins when Jesus said 'It is finished.' The door of that mercy still stands open today — but the throne's 'It is done' warns that it will not stand open forever.

_meta.title: The Seven Bowls of Wrath
reflection: Twice the plagues fall and twice heaven answers not with protest but with worship: 'true and righteous are thy judgments' (v.7). What would it reshape in you to be able to trust God's justice even where you cannot yet see its rightness?

In the middle of the world's gathering for war, Christ turns aside to say, 'Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments' (v.15). What does staying awake and clothed in his righteousness look like on an ordinary day, before any final hour?

Under every plague the refrain returns — they 'repented not' (vv.9, 11, 21). If suffering alone cannot soften a hard heart, and only grace can, where do you need to ask God for the softening he alone gives?
